title: No-Power-Tools Spice Drawer Organizer
summary: A slanted spice-jar rack made from thin wood strips, sized entirely by test-fitting your own drawer and jars.
description:
This organizer has no fixed dimensions at all — every measurement comes from your own drawer and your own spice jars, checked by test-fitting as you go rather than by cutting to numbers from a plan.

Each frame is a simple rectangle of thin strips, but the **spacing between the two long strips is what tilts the jars to a readable angle**. Set that spacing by placing a jar on top of two trial strips and adjusting until the label reads clearly without the jar tipping over or the drawer failing to close — then cut the short connecting pieces to match that gap.

No power tools are required. A fine saw and miter box handle every cut here, which makes this a reasonable first hand-tool project.

tools.note: A fine saw and miter box, or a miter saw if you have one.

steps.title: Measure the drawer
steps.body: Take three measurements off the drawer itself, because this project has no cut list at all and every piece is sized from these numbers. Measure the clear inside width side to side, the clear inside depth front to back, and the vertical clearance — how tall something can stand in the drawer and still let it close under whatever is above it. The last one is the measurement people skip and then discover too late: the strips are only 3/4" x 3/4", so the frame adds 3/4" under the jar, and the jar plus its tilt has to clear the cabinet rail above. Write all three down before you pick up the saw.

steps.title: Set the tilt angle
steps.body: Cut two long strips to the drawer's width, set them in the drawer, and test-fit a spice jar on top of them. Adjust the spacing between the two strips until the jar tilts to a readable angle without blocking the drawer from closing.

steps.title: Cut the connecting pieces
steps.body: Measure the gap between the two long strips you just fitted, and cut two short connecting pieces to that exact length.

steps.title: Glue up a frame
steps.body: Sand all four pieces, then glue the short connecting pieces between the long strips to form a rectangular frame. Clamp until dry.

steps.title: Repeat to fill the drawer
steps.body: Measure the front-to-back size of the finished frame — it is the jar-tilt gap you settled on plus the 3/4" thickness of each of the two long strips — then divide the drawer's inside depth by that figure to work out how many frames will fit. Build that many, cutting every long strip to the same drawer width and every connecting piece to the same gap so the frames are interchangeable and sit in any order. Cut and glue in batches rather than one frame at a time; the clamping and drying is the slow part here, not the sawing.

steps.title: Add spacers and load the drawer
steps.body: Set all the finished frames into the drawer side by side and measure whatever gap is left between the last frame and the drawer side. Cut loose spacer strips to that length and drop them in — they get no glue, just a snug friction fit so nothing shifts when the drawer is opened and closed. Sand the top edges and any sharp corners with the 120 grit so jars slide rather than catch, then load the jars in and check the drawer still closes fully with the tallest jar in place.
